item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image originates from here, which is all rights reserved. As such, it is not compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Umm…. an electric car, out of nowhere, displacing bad milk…

I don't get it. To go even further I don't get why this managed to have more than 10 upvotes.
For this reason I'm staying neutral because I may have missed something other 30 people have not.

And no, I don't think it can fit as an extension of political-parties-made-of-awkward-dolls.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't a more effective containment procedure be to follow 20m behind SCP-1878-1 collecting the milk up and loading it into a van?

Also, it seems that incinerating SCP-1878-1 is pointless if that does not stop it, and seeing as how the worst it can do to the agents is stare at them and talk gibberish, I see no reason to repeatedly kidnap it.

item.title: Nice idea, felt short
item.content:encoded:
Not much to add, many comment about the organization apathy in dealing with the event. Somewhere there is some force that invests great resources to deliver bad milk to children, and the ones that should investigate can't be bothered to. I can think some dozen test to run, starting from following the cart to see where it goes after the delivery tour.
Worse part is the intercept procedure. Seems no one is around at five o'clock to notice the weirdest mafia-style activity in town: big thugs stealing the milkman trolley. As a person that commute to work I would have noticed soon or later, and I'm not the only one going around early in the morning.
Nice idea, felt short.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
██-██-1995: SCP-1878 did not manifest for 13 days.

So this thing comes on a schedule?

I personally don't like this article. Like many people have said before, it goes nowhere. I don't see any purpose, or even anything creepy.

Something delivers free milk that is sometimes rotten. Nothing else about it is notable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At 0445 hrs, assigned operatives must ensure that a Foundation-operated truck (LGV) is in place at the intersection between Alberts Rd. and Downsfield Rd

So every day at 4:45 AM the same intersection is blocked by a truck for 15 minutes? Traffic is going to be very sparse at that time of day, but still, sooner or later someone would catch onto the recurring pattern.

If an instance of SCP-1878 is not neutralised it will proceed to deliver SCP-1878-2 to all houses along Alberts Rd. (located in █████, England) occupied by a family with at least one child under the age of twelve (17 households as of ██-██-████).

If these people have signed up for daily milk delivery, then they're going to complain about the absence of their milk, and the milk delivery company involved will investigate. If they haven't signed up for daily milk delivery, they're going to wonder where in the world this milk came from, and probably won't trust it enough to give it to their children.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upon futher thought I decided to scrap the second addendum, it wasn't really where I had originally wanted to take the SCP anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ya need ta brush up on yo vidya games, son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …It's from Psychonauts. You have a sick mind :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the core concept (the creepy milkman alien from nowhere) but I really dislike, well, everything else about this article. I downvoted hard when I got to the sheer numbers of D-class used to find out something we never get to know (but are supposed to assume is generically horrifying anyway I guess). Meh. Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The amount of D-class required is pretty outrageous - keeping over 500 around for three years, most of them to just drink non-anomalous milk?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good enough for an upvote, but would be better with a little more about what actually happens in the end.
I mean, redaction is ok but the whole thing is a bit too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somewhere, a child plays with a toy town playset. Think Fischer-Price Little People. In this playset is a milk mobile with a little man for a driver. He delivers milk to all the children on Alberts Road. This is the child's fantasy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: correct

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The addition of the test log was enough to bump it into an upvote for me, and (I think) is a good use of a broad [DATA EXPUNGED]

I noticed though, it lists "TEST 1", "TEST 2", and then "TEST 1" again, I'm guessing that was a typo and should have been 3?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the criticism guys. There's definitely room for improvement and I'm going to keep thinking about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Not sure what too think. It irritates me greatly that the last long term experiment is redacted, it seems that experiment is the key to this article and its missing. True I find the remaining material intriguing, but I can't even begin to guess at whats behind that redaction, yet its built up to be the most important part of the SCP.

I would say I like the writing, but the Redaction irritates me too much to upvote. Give me something my imagination can run with and I might change my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't really draw any connection between this and the political puppet one - I think that's tenuous, and it wouldn't improve this for me - but I'm still giving it a hesitant upvote. There's something vaguely unsettling about milk, between its opacity and how quickly it spoils, that this manages to tweak on a little bit. It's hitting a food anxiety in a very queasy way.

The most effective part, and the creepiest part to me, is what happens when the Foundation fails to stop the delivery. Oh, some mysterious person left unmarked bottles of milk on my doorstep? Let me take that inside and feed it to my children. It's not something to do with any kind of compulsive effect; it's just the way people naturally behave. Now that is unsettling.

As far as mechanical criticism goes: I'm in the minority here, I suspect, but I could do without Addendum B, and particularly without the [REDACTED] test, as nothing is really implied to me there as far as what could be happening. Do they turn into more milkmen? Do they grow udders? Do they develop healthy bones and reduce their risk of osteoporosis? You can drop that without losing anything from the article, I feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To me this article comes across as trying too hard to be mysterious, with the end result of it not actually saying anything.

From a canon perspective, it sort of comes across as apathy on the behalf of the researchers involved. They say that they're unable to pinpoint the origin point of the vehicle. Why is that?

The weird anatomy of the driver might be something worth explaining a little bit more, if only to flesh things out a bit.

The new addendum about the milk helps a little, but the redacted at the end feels to me more like a cockblock than something that creates a sense of mystery or dread. It still leaves us with nothing more than a "weird guy who delivers weird milk" as the core of the article.

The premise of the article got me really intrigued, and it feels like there's some cool ideas here, there's just too much behind the curtain and not enough out front.

item.title: Re: Are you serious?
item.content:encoded: That's Echo in a nutshell.

item.title: Re: Are you serious?
item.content:encoded: Wow. That's a lot of anger about people liking something you don't like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminded me of a short tale by Stephen King from the tale book The Skeleton Crew (and The Mist later on).

Where does it come from? Is this the hook?
I'm staying neutral.

This might interest some people here. Animated interpretation of the Morning Deliveries (Milkman) tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I DON'T WANT THIS TO BE THE ACTUAL MEANING OF THIS ARTICLE OH DEAR GOD PLEASE NO TOO FAR

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All of this. It's a situation without any of the things a researcher assigned to write a report on it would investigate. So for me it doesn't fit with the SCP Foundation's situation: a military organisation actively investigating what SCPs are up to to see what threat they pose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Erectin' a… Oh gods.

You monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well it is non-dairy and the original item name was going to be Mann Milk, so… yeah.

BONK!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it came from radioactive cows- now I keep thinking of Scout, ah, having a meeting with himself. Need brain bleach here!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't write it with your article in mind, I've had this sandboxed for ages. That being said, I do like your scp and there are obvious comparisons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't tell us or hint at us anything. Trying to interpret this work would be a lot like when I did criticism class in college.

Hey, since SCP-XXXX-1 is described not to respond to anything, what if it's not actually alive and is just a surrealistic physical ghost that delivers the surrealistic impression of milk? It probably represents how humanity is so discarded from the source of our dairy that we cannot even register the people who deliver them as human. GENIUS!

…

Don't worry, I'll get the noose to hang myself with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not quite. I like 'awkward puppet on a incomprehensible mission' so much that I'm willing to vote for it twice, notwithstanding the fact that this doesn't do anything which SCP-1377 doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While it fits this interpretation, there's nothing in the article that provides evidence for such a link. In light of it, this is coattails riding squared - getting upvotes solely due to a link to another SCP when not only there's nothing interesting in the article, but even the link isn't there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Allow me to rephrase. The reason that some people like this seems to be because it's a continuation of SCP-1377. If it indeed is meant to be that, I think it needs to work a little harder than simply referencing similar features of that article. As I said above, I don't think this stands on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't picked up on any meta-narrative thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that's indeed the case, then the article needs to do a little more to confirm that's what's going on. Again, we get no description as to what the lifeforms that are driving these things are. I feel like the article is relying way too heavily on site meta-narrative to stand on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this myself, but I think I might like it chiefly because I want to believe it is something to do with those things that try to address political bodies because of Ophite's post.

In that light this works just as well because everything that is wrong about this is the fact that it's some weird puppet from out of nowhere that is trying to feign normalcy. And that's enough for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And that was enough for me as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My initial reading was that this was another project by the entities that built the fake Senators. That was enough to warrant my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was written well enough to stave off a downvote brigade, but this article has a lot of threads that don't go anywhere.

For instance, the drivers are some weird life form, with different anatomy and somesuch, but beyond a passing mention we don't get any info on them. You would think that would be a fairly important piece to understanding this phenomenon that the Foundation would want to document.

Also, this "milk" stuff is some unknown substance that's being distributed to a particular neighborhood for some reason. You would think that the Foundation would want to know why it's being fed to people, but instead the article just goes "eh, we don't know what long-term exposure would do." Seriously, you can't feed it repeatedly to some random D-Class and see what happens? Even if it was nothing, the Foundation would probably investigate that.

There's other loose ends, too. Do these things just materialize out of thin air? The phenomenon is extremely localized, it doesn't seem like it would be out of line to observe the area to see where, exactly, these are coming from. And why is the battery mentioned? Is that important somehow?

What this reads like, to me, is a ton of little odd threads that have been thrown together without a lot of care to building a cohesive vision. While it's true that one of the hallmarks of this site is letting the reader scare/interest/amuse themselves with their own imagination, you have to provide some direction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I… Do not see what the author is doing here. I just find the thing rather bland, harmless, and uninteresting. Well written though. Maybe if there was a bit more there, some sort of ACTUAL implication or subtle nod, but if it's there it's SO subtle as to be almost invisible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's all milk and no cereal, doesn't feel like there's any substance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slightly salty, yet full of protein.

item.title: Re: Are you serious?
item.content:encoded:
Thirding this - it's a Potemkin article, just a facade with nothing going on behind.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I really disliked this, but a subsequent readthrough made me see what you were doing here. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, Mad Milk comes from the Scout himself (think of it like Jarate, but white and nutritious.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this where Mad Milk comes from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thermonuclear radiation?

item.title: Re: Are you serious?
item.content:encoded: While these are fair points, a lot of SCP articles do rely on allowing the readers to draw their own conclusions. I tried to drop some subtle hints in the article, of course you are welcome to disagree. I think there's a fine line between being too subtle and revealing just enough, I may well be on the wrong side of that line.

item.title: Re: Are you serious?
item.content:encoded: Wholeheartedly agreed. You are making a judgement based on what you've wildly assumed, not anything the author has actually written, which doesn't make this article redeemable.

item.title: Are you serious?
item.content:encoded: Christ Almighty, half the people here have must have critical thinking and reading skills as poor as the writing that usually gets shoveled up. He doesn't imply shit in the article. It's all an exercise in meta, with no hints to prod the reader in that direction. There's a difference between hinting at something and simply being quiet and hoping something is interpreted one way or another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's fortified with what the world wants. What the world deserves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I originally used neutralised but someone suggested that the common meaning of the word on the wiki could cause confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it! I especially like how you hint that the "milk" is pretty fucked up but don't state exactly what it does.
Only a few minor problems with the first paragraph
use "neutralized" instead of "nullified"
no need to state "All times are in GMT" unless the procedure can be initiated from a remote location outside of the local timezone.
I think that's it, nice job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have a decent setup for a SCP, but then it falls straight off a cliff. There's no hook whatsoever, nothing that leaves you wondering what is actually in the milk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am the milkman. My milk is delicious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, it's there now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ratings module?
